These substances have to be removed totally or partially. In... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: EXCRETORY PRODUCTS AND THEIR ELIMINATION 205, EXCRETORY PRODUCTS AND, THEIR ELIMINATION, CHAPTER 16. full_notes:
Digestion and Absorption is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that eXCRETORY PRODUCTS AND THEIR ELIMINATION 205 Animals accumulate ammonia, urea, uric acid, carbon dioxide, water and ions like Na+, K+, Cl–, phosphate, sulphate, etc., either by metabolic activities or by other means like excess ingestion.

The text explains that these substances have to be removed totally or partially.

The text explains that in this chapter, you will learn the mechanisms of elimination of these substances with special emphasis on common nitrogenous wastes.

The text explains that ammonia, urea and uric acid are the major forms of nitrogenous wastes excreted by the animals.

The text explains that ammonia is the most toxic form and requires large amount of water for its elimination, whereas uric acid, being the least toxic, can be removed with a minimum loss of water.

The text explains that the process of excreting ammonia is Ammonotelism.

The text explains that many bony fishes, aquatic amphibians and aquatic insects are ammonotelic ammonotelic ammonotelic ammonotelic ammonotelic in nature.

The text explains that ammonia, as it is readily soluble, is generally excreted by diffusion across body surfaces or through gill surfaces (in fish) as ammonium ions.

The text explains that kidneys do not play any significant role in its removal.

The text explains that terrestrial adaptation necessitated the production of lesser toxic nitrogenous wastes like urea and uric acid for conservation of water.

The text explains that mammals, many terrestrial amphibians and marine fishes mainly excrete urea and are called ureotelic ureotelic ureotelic ureotelic ureotelic animals.

The text explains that ammonia produced by metabolism is converted into urea in the liver of these animals and released into the blood which is filtered and excreted out by the kidneys.

The text explains that some amount of urea may be retained in the kidney matrix of some of these animals to maintain a desired osmolarity.

The text explains that reptiles, birds, land snails and insects excrete nitrogenous wastes as uric acid in the form of pellet or paste with a minimum loss of water and are called uricotelic uricotelic uricotelic uricotelic uricotelic animals.

The text explains that eXCRETORY PRODUCTS AND THEIR ELIMINATION CHAPTER 16 16.1 Human Excretory System 16.2 Urine Formation 16.3 Function of the Tubules 16.4 Mechanism of Concentration of the Filtrate 16.5 Regulation of Kidney Function 16.6 Micturition 16.7 Role of other Organs in Excretion 16.8 Disorders of the Excretory System

The text explains that 206 BIOLOGY A survey of animal kingdom presents a variety of excretory structures.

The text explains that in most of the invertebrates, these structures are simple tubular forms whereas vertebrates have complex tubular organs called kidneys.

The text explains that protonephridia or flame cells are the excretory structures in Platyhelminthes (Flatworms, e.g., Planaria), rotifers, some annelids and the cephalochordate – Amphioxus.

The text explains that nephridia are the tubular excretory structures of earthworms and other annelids.

The text explains that malpighian tubules are the excretory structures of most of the insects including cockroaches.

The text explains that antennal glands or green glands perform the excretory function in crustaceans like prawns.

The text explains that 16.1 HUMAN EXCRETORY SYSTEM In humans, the excretory system consists of a pair of kidneys, one pair of ureters, a urinary bladder and a urethra (Figure 16.1).

The text explains that kidneys are reddish brown, bean shaped structures situated between the levels of last thoracic and third lumbar vertebra close to the dorsal inner wall of the abdominal cavity.

The text explains that the cortex extends in between the Figure 16.1 Human Urinary system

The text explains that each kidney has nearly one million complex tubular structures called nephrons (Figure 16.3), which are the functional units.

The text explains that the renal tubule begins with a double walled cup-like structure called Bowman’s capsule, which encloses the glomerulus.

The text explains that 16.2 URINE FORMATION Urine formation involves three main processes namely, glomerular filtration, reabsorption and secretion, that takes place in different parts of the nephron.

The text explains that the glomerular capillary blood pressure causes filtration of blood through 3 layers, i.e., the endothelium of glomerular blood vessels, the epithelium of Bowman’s capsule and a basement membrane between these two layers.

The text explains that therefore, it is considered as a process of ultra filtration.

The text explains that the flow of filtrate in the two limbs of Henle’s loop is in opposite directions and thus forms a counter current.

The text explains that the flow of blood through the two limbs of vasa recta is Figure 16.5 Reabsorption and secretion of major substances at different parts of the nephron (Arrows indicate direction of movement of materials.)

The text explains that an increase in blood pressure can increase the glomerular blood flow and thereby the GFR.

The text explains that a fall in glomerular blood flow/glomerular blood pressure/GFR can activate the JG cells to release renin which converts angiotensinogen in blood to angiotensin I and further to angiotensin II.

The text explains that angiotensin II, being a powerful vasoconstrictor, increases the glomerular blood pressure and thereby GFR.

The text explains that this also leads to an increase in blood pressure and GFR.

The text explains that aNF can cause vasodilation (dilation of blood vessels) and thereby decrease the blood pressure.

The text explains that 16.6 MICTURITION Urine formed by the nephrons is ultimately carried to the urinary bladder where it is stored till a voluntary signal is given by the central nervous system (CNS).

The text explains that the process of release of urine is called micturition and the neural mechanisms causing it is called the micturition reflex.

The text explains that 16.8 DISORDERS OF THE EXCRETORY SYSTEM Malfunctioning of kidneys can lead to accumulation of urea in blood, a condition called uremia, which is highly harmful and may lead to kidney failure.

The text explains that in such patients, urea can be removed by a process called hemodialysis.

The text explains that during the process of haemodialysis, the blood drained from a convenient artery is pumped into a dialysing unit called artificial kidney.

The text explains that in humans, the excretory system consists of one pair of kidneys, a pair of ureters, a urinary bladder and a urethra.

The text explains that each kidney has over a million tubular structures called nephrons.

The text explains that urine formation involves three main processes, i.e., filtration, reabsorption and secretion.

The text explains that filtration is a non-selective process performed by the glomerulus using the glomerular capillary blood pressure.

The text explains that a functioning kidney is used in transplantation from a donor, preferably a close relative, to minimise its chances of rejection by the immune system of the host.

The text explains that name the following: (a) A chordate animal having flame cells as excretory structures (b) Cortical portions projecting between the medullary pyramids in the human kidney (c) A loop of capillary running parallel to the Henle’s loop.

The text explains that give a brief account of the counter current mechanism.

The text explains that describe the role of liver, lungs and skin in excretion.

The text explains that match the items of column I with those of column II : Column I Column II (a) Ammonotelism (i) Birds (b) Bowman’s capsule (ii) Water reabsorption (c) Micturition (iii) Bony fish (d) Uricotelism (iv) Urinary bladder (d) ADH (v) Renal tubule

The text explains that terrestrial animals are generally either ureotelic or uricotelic, not ammonotelic, why ?

The text explains that what is the significance of juxta glomerular apparatus (JGA) in kidney function?

Source: NCERT Class 11 Biology, Chapter 16: Digestion and Absorption

cases.name: FUNCTION OF THE TUBULES Proximal
cases.point: 16.3 FUNCTION OF THE TUBULES Proximal Convoluted Tubule (PCT): PCT is lined by simple cuboidal brush border epithelium which increases the surface area for reabsorption. (NCERT Class 11 Biology Ch16 pg 4)

cases.name: Distal Convoluted Tubule
cases.point: Distal Convoluted Tubule (DCT): Conditional reabsorption of Na+ and water takes place in this segment. (NCERT Class 11 Biology Ch16 pg 4)
